videos.transcript: In this video, we'll show you the top 10 things to do in Lisbon. This video is based on our fun trip to this beautiful city. Don't forget to like this video, subscribe to our channel, and enable notifications. And share your own experience or ask a question in the comments below. Sponsored by Beeyond compression packing cubes for travel. The link is in the description. And stick around until the end because we have a bonus attraction for you. Here are our top 10 picks: Number 10: Belém Tower The Belem Tower, part of UNESCO World Heritage, is one of the most recognizable tourist attractions in Lisbon. Built in the 16th century, it was considered the gateway to the nation's capital, strategically located at the mouth of the Tagus River. It was initially built as a fort to protect the city from incoming raids along the river. The tower is also a symbol of Europe's Age of Discovery, also known as Age of Exploration, a period in the 15th and 16th century where some European nations, including Portugal, began exploring and conquering the world. Did you know that the tower once stood on an island, but the earthquake of 1755 shifted the river's course, moving the island closer to land? Don't skip nearby attractions including the statue celebrating the Portuguese Age of Discovery, and Waterfront Promenade Belém with a fantastic view towards the beautiful 25 de Abril bridge Number 9: Old-style forms of public transportation In the late 18-hundreds and the beginning of 20th century, Lisbon came up with several ingenious transportation solutions to help locals and visitors tackle the city’s steep hills and enjoy its magnificent sights. Trams are a popular way to ger around the city, nowadays especially amongst tourists. These small nostalgic streetcars have been a part of Lisbon’s streets since 1901. Ride the renowned tram number 28 that passes through the old city and other famous parts of Lisbon. By the way, did you know that before 1901 trams ran as a horsecar line, but due to Lisbon's steep slopes, the horses were eventually replaced by a more efficient cable-driven transport. Funiculars are another fun way to explore Lisbon. Ascensor do Lavra, city’s first funicular, was inaugurated in 1884. There are others, like Elevador da Glória famous for its colorful street art, or Bica funicular that will take you through the picturesque part of the city. Ride the neo-Gothic vertical lift Elevador de Santa Justa connecting downtown and Chiado, or take advantage of the modern ones, like Lift Castelo. Number 8: Castel of Saint George Castel of Saint George, initially surrounded by a wall to form a citadel, is now an iconic historical complex that played an essential role in the history of Lisbon. The fortification dates back to the 1st century AD and was occupied by different civilizations, however, the area was inhabited long before. The site was eventually conquered from the Moors by the Portuguese military action in the 1147 Siege of Lisbon. In the 13th century, the castle became a fortified residence for the kings. Today, visitors can enjoy a walk-through of castle's expansive grounds that include the ruins of the royal palace, a permanent exhibition with objects found in the archaeological area, and several platforms with amazing panoramic views of the city and other attractions. Of course, you cannot miss funny and loud peacocks roaming around the castle complex, a sort of unofficial castle permanent residents. Check our Lisbon walking tour to get the real feel of the castle and the entire city. The link is in the description. Number 7: Mercado da Ribeira and other food destinations One does not fully explore Lisbon without tasting the city's unique cuisine. Mercado da Ribeira is one of Lisbon's oldest markets and has been around since the 13th century. From the early beginning, the market has undergone several upgrades. The market hall, which features iron interiors and a large oriental dome, was opened in 1882. The area is divided into the traditional market that sells fresh produce and a modern food hall with over 30 stalls and bars serving top-notch Portuguese cuisine as well as food from other parts of the world. In 2014, the Time Out Market, a venture of Time Out magazine, took over the food hall part of Mercado da Ribeira. Don't skip other markets and food destinations across the city, like the bohemian Lx Factory situated in an old textile factory complex, now full of trendy bars and restaurants, quirky shops, independent art studios and Livraria Ler Devagar – one of the most unique bookstores in the world. Walk around cobblestone streets, sit down in one of many bars or restaurants, and soak up the atmosphere. Explore Alfama and other districts with traditional taverns and restaurants, some even offering live fado, a unique genre of music that originated in Lisbon. Check our travel guide for more suggestions. By the way, our mobile-friendly travel guide covers the top 20 things to do in Lisbon and things to know before you visit, including maps, opening hours, links to buy tickets, itinerary suggestions, and other information. By purchising our travel guide, you are also helping us sustain this channel, so a big thank you for that! Number 6: Carmo Convent Built in the 14th century, Carmo Convent used to be the largest church in Lisbon. But the Great Earthquake of 1755 destroyed the building, along with much of the city. Today, visitors can admire its grand arches and the carvings on the walls that have survived for centuries. A small archeological museum located at the sacristy features archeological finds from Portuguese history, including fountains, tombs, and architectural relics from different styles and areas. The museum also features a 15-minute interactive video projection describing the convent's history. Number 5: Lisbon Zoo No matter your age, a trip to the zoo will always be fun and memorable, and especially so at the Lisbon Zoo. The zoo founded in 1884, is the first park with various flora and fauna in the Iberian Peninsula. Today, Lisbon Zoo is home to over 2,000 animals from 300 different species from all over the globe, including dolphins, tigers, reptiles, primates, and other animals. The mission of the zoo is also scientific research, conservation and breeding of endangered species, along with the recreational activities. Embark on a unique adventure and explore the zoo from a different perspective and jump on a fascinating 20-minutes cable car ride. Number 4: Museum of Art, Architecture, and Technology Visit The Museum of Art, Architecture, and Technology or MAAT opened in 2016. As the name suggests, it is home to exhibitions in art, architecture, and technology. Its distinct design stands out, with its prominent location on the banks of the River Tagus. The museum’s rooftop offers a fantastic views of the 25 de Abril bridge and the city. Instead of permanent collections, visitors can explore temporary exhibits by contemporary artists, and other creators like architects, both national and international. Don't skip Tejo Power Station, situated in a former thermoelectric power plant, now a permanent exhibition venue. Lisbon has a long and rich history, and this is reflected in its numerous museums. Check our travel guide for more suggestions. Number 3: Jerónimos Monastery Construction of this massive monastery and a church for the Order of Saint Jerome began in 1501 but was not completed for another 100 years. Jerónimos Monastery replaced the old church used by monks providing assistance to sailors in transit. The monastery is a prime example of Manueline architectural style from the 16th century. This Portuguese late Gothic style originated in the Portuguese Renaissance and Age of Discoveries. The style incorporates richly decorated maritime elements carved in limestone discovered on expeditions of Vasco da Gama and Pedro Álvares Cabral. Stroll along the two-story cloisters and the Church of Santa Maria, and it will be easy to understand why Jerónimos Monastery is considered a symbol of Portugal's immense wealth during the Age of Discoveries. The monastery, now a UNESCO World Heritage Site, is also a burial place of King Manuel I, and other prominent figures, like the famous Portuguese explorer Vasco da Gama. Did you know that the recipe for the world-renowned Portuguese custard tarts known as pastéis de Nata also called pastéis de Belém was created by the monks of Jerónimos Monastery? This video is sponsored by Beeyond, helping you save space when you travel and organize your suitcase. A revolutionary new way to organize your luggage consists of a set of small and large packing cubes. Once you're done packing, just close both zippers, compress the air out of your packing cubes like this, and voila, your clothes are compressed, and your luggage is organized. We use Beeyond packing cubes on our travels, and they are even designed to fit your carry-on. Visit Beeyond's Amazon page to get your own compression packing cube set. The link is in the description. Number 2: Oceanário de Lisboa Oceanário de Lisboa is the largest indoor aquarium in Europe, home to over 16,000 marine animals and plants from 450 different species, including sandtiger sharks, cute sea otters, funny penguins, and other fascinating animals. Walk around this unique aquarium and experience four different natural habitats and learn about animal life in their natural environment through information panels. The oceanarium's main exhibit is a large tank with glass panel windows strategically placed in various aquarium parts, creating a unique visual experience. Oceanário de Lisboa is just one of the famous landmarks located in Parque das Nações, the former site of Expo '98. While in the Expo district, admire the majestic Vasco da Gama bridge, the longest bridge in Europe, or the beautiful Torre Vasco da Gama. Hop on the cable car to get a nice view of the entire district. Number 1: Commerce Square Praça do Comércio or Commerce Square, overlooking the Tagus River, served as a transportation and commercial hub, and is now the seat of some of the most important Portuguese state offices. Walk along Cais das Colunas or Ribeira das Naus to enjoy a historic waterfront area. One of the most famous attractions in the square is Arco da Rua Augusta, a stone building commemorating the city's reconstruction after the earthquake in 1755. Climb the arch to enjoy spectacular views of the square and the city. The arch entrance reveals Lisbon's main pedestrian area, Rua Augusta, a bustling street full of shops and restaurants. The nearby Rossio Square has been one of the main squares in Lisbon since the Middle Ages. Sit down in one of many cafes or restaurants, like the historic Café Nicola, and admire traditional Portuguese pavement style "calçada" and the plaza's baroque fountains and neoclassical architecture. And here is the bonus that we promised. If you have some time left, explore Almada district with Cristo Rei National Sanctuary, a favorite destination for those looking to spend some quiet time with breathtaking views towards the river and the city. If you find it familiar, it’s because it resembles the Christ the Redeemer statue of Rio de Janeiro. Thank you for watching. We wish you an amazing trip to Lisbon!

infrastructure is really really old old buildings not a lot of accessible roads uneven surfaces and lots and lots of hills but you're going to get a lot of charm now I see a lot of tourists staying in two areas bisha and chiato to me bisha it's a total tourist trap it's the place that I wouldn't recommend for anybody yes it is Central and yes it is flat you're not going to deal with too many Hills but to be honest everything around there's a tourist trap I wouldn't try staying there chato is a little bit different there are going to be a few Hills you're still really Central to everything it's got a little bit more Local Charm but there are definitely going to be some tourist traps in there as well it's probably not for me but if you're just wanting convenience chiato would be a wonderful spot for you another neighborhood that people really like is bario Alto now I'd really recommend that for two groups of people people who like to party and people who are young at heart and are cool with the party atmosphere a lot of people are going to be partying on the streets late at night too so it's going to be pretty loud I wouldn't recommend it for families another area that is close by to there is kaas dadre that's just below bario Alto now it also has a bit of a party scene definitely that young demographic as well but it's hugged right up against the river you get really good access to everything else and you don't have to tackle too many Hills unless you're going straight up to bario ala or principal real so I really like that area I found myself hanging out there quite a bit and the other place is Principe real which is all the way at the top of the hill you're going to have a more affluent Community up there definitely some rich folks really Chic boho I don't know it's a really nice place definitely worth checking out if you've got the money and you want to say hey I want to start at the top of the hill each day and work my way down and then maybe I'll take a bolt back up might be a great spot for you but probably not where I'd be looking to stay my next time now if you want to know where I'm going to base myself the next time that I'm visiting well that's actually what I was referring to about my biggest mistake while Lisbon is spectacular we ended up loving Porto so much more and if you want to know everything that I wish I knew before visiting Porto you can watch this video right here or if you want an unedited unfiltered conversation about my experience living in Portugal for a month you can watch this video right here I hope this was helpful and we'll catch you guys next time

videos.transcript: [Music] ready to discover the best of Lisbon in just 3 days join us as we explore the vibrant streets of Portugal's capital from the historic Bellum Tower to the stunning views at Mirador dos and Yura dumante we'll uncover hidden gems like the enchanting alfama district and treat your taste buds to the city's culinary Delights stay till the end for exclusive Insider tips to make your trip Unforgettable don't forget to subscribe for more exciting travel itineraries and Adventures let's dive into [Music] Lisbon best time to visit Lisbon Lisbon enjoys a Mediterranean climate with mild rainy Winters and hot dry Summers the best time to visit is during the Spring march to May or fall September to October when the weather is pleasant and the tourist Crowds Are thinner summer is Peak tourist season with festivals and events but it can be crowded and hot winter is less crowded and more affordable but be prepared for some rainy days lisban airport tips lisban airport Humberto Delgado airport is the main gateway to the city it's well connected to the city center just 4.3 M away upon arrival you can take the Arrow bus a shuttle service that costs around 4 USD for a one-way trip or 7 USD for a round trip the Metro is another option costing approximately 2 USD and taxis are available for about 15 to 20 USD be sure to have some euros handy for initial expenses where to stay in Lisbon for an authentic Lisbon experience consider staying in the alphama district known for its narrow streets and Futo music here you can find Charming boutique hotels and guest houses ranging from 80 to 150 USD per night for those preferring a more modern touch baa and shato offer luxury hotels and contemporary amenities with prices averaging 150 to 250 USD per night byro Alto is perfect for nightlife enthusiasts with vibrant bars and restaurants though it can be noisier how to get around Lisbon Lisbon's transportation system is both efficient and affordable the Viva via gy card is the musthave offering Unlimited travel on buses trams and the Metro for around 7 USD per day alternatively the lisban card provides free entry to many attractions and unlimited transport for 22 USD for 24 hours for a unique experience ride the iconic tram 28 which costs about 4 USD per trip walking is also a great way to explore Lisbon's hilly terrain and discover hidden gems day one explore alph District early morning sunrise at Mid de Santa Lucia one of my favorite spots in all of Lisbon is the Mirador de Santa Lua it's absolutely magical at Sunrise watching the Sun come over the water transforming the sky from a deep red to a vibrant orange is a breathtaking way to start your day in Lisbon trust me getting up early for this view is totally worth it an added bonus of visiting at Sunrise is that you'll beat the crowds that gather here later in the day plus you can enjoy the Tranquility of this beautiful spot almost all to yourself breakfast after soaking in the breathtaking sunrise at madoro De Santa Lua head to pastur Cafe conveniently located nearby for breakfast try the tosta Mista a Portuguese ham and cheese toasty paired with a fresh orange juice and a coffee costing around 10 USD morning M naal AO the national tile museum is one of my absolute favorites in Lisbon it's entirely dedicated to the fascinating history of tile work in Portugal showcasing studying azuos from different eras our highlight was the panoramic tile painting of Lisbon viewed from the water we had a blast spotting various landmarks we had visited during our trip inside or tip the museum is located in a former Convent adding to its charm entry costs about 5 USD and it's a great way to spend a couple hours especially on a rainy day don't forget to check out the Museum's beautiful Courtyard and Cafe for a relaxing break explore alpham neighborhood the alpham neighborhood is a Charming area with stunning views of the tagus river this historic district is home to madoro De Santa luia and the portas DUS two of the best spots for panoramic views of the city alph is also the heart and soul of foto music you'll often hear its Soulful Melodies wafting through the streets especially along Ru de sal Pedro the best time to experience foto is during where you can enjoy a 3-hour feast with live performances the atmosphere is especially magical on weekends Insider tip for an authentic Futo experience head to one of the traditional Futo houses in ALA they usually offer a set menu with the dinner and performance for around 50 USD per person be sure to make a reservation in advance to get the best seats lunch for a delightful lunch in alphama head to Mesa de frades a Charming restaurant located in a former Chapel adorned with beautiful Azo tiles start with the uas Abul Hal clams in a garlic and cilantro sauce for around 10 USD followed by the bakala brass shredded cod with eggs and potatoes a classic Portuguese dish costing about 18 USD Insider tip try to get a table by the window for a cozy experience with a view of alphus charming streets and remember to make a reservation as it can get busy especially during weekends afternoon convento deasa and madoro deasa the convento deasa also known as the Church of Our Lady of Grace is one of Lisbon's oldest convents perched on one of the city's highest hills it offers stunning panoramic views of Lisbon and the tagus River from the madoro De Gracia right in front of it best of all there's no entrance fee the convent itself is quite small so you only need about 30 minutes to explore it Mysterio of sa vincenti Deora in ALA be sure to check out the moero de Vincent Deora this Monastery not only boasts stunning tile work but also offers breathtaking views from its rooftop viewpoints I recommend visiting towards the end of the day because the view over Lisbon from the church towers included in your ticket is as spectacular as any other madoro the monastery closes at 6:00 p.m. with the last ticket sales at 5 p.m. you can get your ticket at the admissions desk for around 5 1/2 USD early evening sunset at pora dool madoro the PTO dool madoro is a beautiful terrist Viewpoint in the heart of alph perched on one of Lisbon's many Hills for an unforgettable experience head here at Sunset and grab a glass of Vino Verde from quos dport doou for about 5 USD although it gets crowded at Sunset with a bit of patience you'll be able to snap some great photos and even find a table with a view to relax before dinner dinner at boab babau is a fantastic Pan Asian restaurant located in the Lively birro Alto neighborhood of Lisbon I loved the vibe and food so much that we ended up going there twice during our trip some of our favorite dishes included the bow buns especially the seab bass bow trust me it sounds unusual but tastes incredible the appetizer platter with a variety of delicious starters and any of the noodle dishes on the menu expect to spend around 20 to 30 USD per person for a satisfying meal boaa doesn't take reservations for lunch or dinner because it's so popular people start lining up before the restaurant even opens for dinner I recommend arriving about 20 to 30 minutes before they open to snag a table without a long wait day two The balim District breakfast if you're researching Lisbon chances are you've come across the delightful custard pastry the pastel danata we've tried our fair share of these little cups of heaven and you might be wondering where can you find the best pastel Dena in Lisbon let me Spill the Beans the best pastel denata we've ever had was at fabria Dena for breakfast they offer a combo where you can get a ham and cheese croissant one pastel dinata and a latte for about 4 USD the history of pastel dinata dates back to the moero do geranos so naturally the nearby BM neighborhood is home to the famous past deim which attracts many visitors every day however I wasn't impressed with their pastel denata the sheer number of visitors seems to affect the quality of the pastry and the atmosphere but that's just my opinion Insider tip be sure to add a little sprinkle of cinnamon on your pastel dinata and enjoy this gem more than once if you can morning palaso National de BM and gonos Monastery BM is a Charming neighborhood about 5 miles west of lizbon City Center nestled along the Waterfront it's home to some of Lisbon's most iconic landmarks including cluding the Tower of BM BM Palace and gonos Monastery these attractions are popular stops for cruise ships so it's best to arrive early in the morning to fully appreciate the stunning Portuguese architecture without the crowds the easiest way to get to BM is a 15-minute taxi ride which will cost you around 10 to 15 USD alternatively you can take a short bus or train ride for just a few dollars Insider tip if you visit BM in the morning don't miss grabbing a freshly baked past de BM from the famous bakery nearby it's the perfect treat to start your day lunch not far from BM sud booa is a modern restaurant and event center with an unbeatable view of the tagos river we had a delightful lunch on the patio enjoying fresh appetizers an oxtail Ragu and a seafood pasta all of which were incredibly fresh and delicious while this restaurant isn't the cheapest option it's perfect for a treatyourself meal by the water and worth every penny expect to spend around 50 to 70 usd per person for a meal here afternoon take a sailboat out on the water one of our most memorable experiences in Lisbon was taking a sailboat cruise on the tagus river the sailing tour usually includes wine beer and snacks and if you're lucky the captain might even give you a bit of sailing instruction I highly recommend booking a sailing tour over one of the larger boats because you'll get a much more intimate and local experience these sailboats typically hold a maximum of 10 people to book a similar experience check out these highly rated sailing tours with a local guide they're flexible too travel plans can be unpredictable so you can cancel up to 24 hours in advance for a full refund Insider tip expect to spend around 50 to 70 usd per person for this incredible experience book your tour for the late afternoon to catch a breathtaking Sunset over Lisbon from the water dinner pra is an experience plain and simple the Ambiance is casual warm and Lively all at once they have a fantastic farmtable concept using only seasonal and local market ingredients the food is unlike anything we've ever experienced my husband usually box at fancy dining saying the portions are small and it's all about presentation so he was definitely skeptical going into our meal at PR but let me tell you he left feeling full and satisfied with every dish we ordered while the presentation was impeccable the steak tartar served on a crispy kale chip had unbelievable flavor our favorite dish though was a carbonara like creation where the noodles were impossibly tender squid lending an insane flavor profile to the dish we are still raving about it meals at prao typically cost around 50 to 70 usd per person depending on what you order you pretty much can't get in without a reservation so make sure to book well in advance day three disc covering CRA so yes there are plenty of other things to do in Lisbon but for this third day I suggest you visit CRA from Lisbon because it's simply a mustsee if you're in the city for 3 Days in my opinion it's about a 30-minute drive from Central Lisbon to CRA you can get there by car or take a train which takes around 40 minutes if you opt for the Lisboa card the train journey is included in the pass which costs about 22 USD for a 24-hour pass on your day trip make sure to explore the stunning Penna Palace and the castle of the Moors both sites are truly breathtaking and will make your trip to Lisbon truly Unforgettable Insider tip start your day early to beat the crowds at these popular attractions and wear comfortable shoes as there's quite a bit of walking involved also consider packing a picnic to enjoy in the beautiful gardens surrounding the Pena Palace morning the morish castle We Begin our visit at the Chateau Des Morris off offering stunning views of the surrounding area this castle built in the 9th century by the Moors from mreb was originally used to monitor the city of cintra today only ruins remain since it was abandoned after the reconquest of Portugal wandering along the forested Ram parts feels almost like being on the Great Wall of China the visit takes about an hour and it's a peaceful and Scenic walk the castle is open 7 days a week from 9:30 a.m. to 8:00 p.m. during the summer season lunch for a delicious lunch near the morish castle check out Tuscan a cozy spot right in the heart of CRA kick off your meal with a PO de Sardinia sardine Pate for about 6 USD then dive into bakala brass shredded cod with eggs and potatoes a traditional Portuguese favorite for around 14 USD wash it all down with a refreshing glass of Vino ver green wine for 5 USD and don't forget to treat yourself to a slice of quada De cintra the traditional cintra cheesecake for about 5 USD you're looking at roughly 30 USD per person afternoon Penna Palace after enjoying your lunch a visit to pen Palace in cintra is an absolute must this Palace is incredibly vibrant and unique making it feel like something straight out of a fairy tale you wouldn't expect such a colorful Palace in the middle of Portugal both the interior and exterior of pen Palace are worth exploring it's the most visited Palace in Portugal attracting nearly a million tourists a year year surrounded by a lush Park it's a delightful place for a walk and you can even take a Scenic path through the park to get back down to the town of Sentra plan to spend about 2 and 1/2 hours to fully explore the palace inside and out I recommend taking the bus up to the palace and then walking down through Penna Park the walk is lovely with shaded paths thousands of flowers forested areas and small lakes it's really Charming for your visit to cintra it's best to set aside an entire day between exploring the pal and traveling you'll have a full and exciting day entry to pen Palace and its Park isn't included in the Lisboa card but you can get a small discount around 10% if you present the pass at the ticket office given the palace's popularity it's a good idea to buy your tickets in advance you have two options you can either purchase a standard ticket or opt for a guided tour that includes cintra and other attractions guided tours usually depart directly from Lisbon and offer a convenient way to explore more of the area Insider tip to avoid long lines and ensure you get to see everything buy your tickets online ahead of time expect to pay around 15 USD for a standard ticket dinner when you return in the evening I advise you go to aeta this little seichi restaurant has been on my list for years and I was thrilled to finally visit it on our latest trip to Lisbon they whip up multiple fresh Ceviche dishes and other fresh seafood quinoa dishes that we thoroughly enjoy not only is the food superb but the large octopus hanging from the ceiling makes for some great pictures expect to spend around 25 to 35 USD per person for a meal they don't take reservations for dinner so to avoid the crowds and get a table we made this a dinner spot arriving about 10 minutes before they opened if you're set on having dinner here show up early and plan to wait it out thanks for joining us on this incredible 3-day journey through Lisbon we hoped you enjoyed discovering the city's Rich history breathtaking views and delicious cuisine as much as we did remember the magic of Lisbon lies in its hidden gems and vibrant culture so be sure to take your time and soak it all in if you found this itinerary helpful give us a thumbs up and share your own Lisbon experiences in the comments below don't forget to subscribe to our channel for more travel guides and Insider tips until next time happy travels [Music]

We want you to have a great time when you're here and I think the first mistake people make is they walk in the Baixa district or they walk in Bairro Alto. They engage with the guys that start with this, "Oh, would you like some sunglasses? Maybe some hashish? Cocaína?" Look, don't engage with those people. Just say no and walk on by because they'll just ask somebody else, okay? So just know that. Some of the locals don't think those people exist just because they actually focus on male tourists and younger tourists. That's who they usually ask. So don't get involved with them, all right? Now the second mistake I see tourists make when they come to Lisbon is they bring the wrong shoes. Look, you have to understand, the streets here, the sidewalks here made of these little cobblestones. They're all put together and it's cool to see cuz they put different designs in there. You'll see all kinds of different things say what street you're on or or what this place sells or just a fancy design. But the thing is, those can be very, very slick when it gets a little wet or a little damp, okay? So you got to be careful. But also, it's a lot of uneven parts and I've seen a number of tourists slip and fall over the years cuz they didn't have the right shoes to have a good grip. So, you know, don't make the mistake of not bringing some shoes you can have some really good grip in so you can go up those beautiful hills and take in the castle on this side or the lookouts on over here in Bairro Alto. And those hills are another thing I think is important to remember. Um before you come here, don't make the mistake of practicing, you know, walking on flat land. You need to go to the gym and get the super high incline stepper and start doing that because you're going to be doing a lot of steps when you're here cuz I know why I weighed 100 lb less and had a perfect butt when I lived here in Portugal because I was walking up and down these hills over and over again and you as a tourist will do it as well. Now when you go around Lisbon, there's all kinds of great districts to go out. You have Baixa down below, you have got over there with the hills, but probably the one that's the most historic that people like to go to and see certain sites is Belém. I know, everybody sells it around town. They sell the Pastel de Nata, but the original is at the Pastéis de Belém, okay? So, don't pass that one up. Also, a bonus don't for that, don't worry about that huge line you see right up front. You can actually walk around cuz that's the to-go line. And when you come out of the monastery, you can go across the street and you see the Monument of Discovery. And then you walk down the way and you see the Torre of Belém, Tower of Belém, okay? You go there, it is beautiful. You've seen all the postcards, you'll see that. That's nice. Um, I don't think you really need to go up it. I've been up it enough times. It's not really anything great. It's more beautiful to look from the outside than the inside, so there is that. But, you you don't skip out on those four things when you go to Belém. And the thing is there's more stuff in Belém. There's multiple museums that are there as well you can check out. So, you can have that. So, don't pass up that district because it's not on the metro line. You either take, you know, a car there or a taxi there or you can take the the train line that goes from Cais do Sodré out to Cascais on the city beaches. You take that to get out there, okay? So, don't pass it up. And when you come to Lisbon, you're going to eat well, okay? So, there's not really any mistakes with the eating when you're here, specifically about the food stuff, but there are some mistakes I see tourists make when it comes to planning out their eating and scheduling their eating and that is they make the mistake of thinking they can eat anytime. Now, if you go into the Baixa district, there's a lot of tourist restaurants that are down there and yes, those you can eat anytime you want, it's no big deal. But traditional tascas, okay, like that's the mom and pop restaurants, they're going to be closing their kitchens between probably 3:00 in the afternoon till 7:00 at night, maybe 8:00 at night at some of them. So, do be aware of that and plan your eating around that cuz you want to have the traditional food when you're here, right? How do you go to those restaurants? Like I There's a mistake I want to make sure you realize. Um don't make the mistake of actually just starting to chow down on those little treats they leave for you, whether it's the the pastéis de bacalhau, the bolinhos de bacalhau, the little codfish cakes, or the bread, or the olives because those you pay for. And when you get the menu, look inside it, then they'll give you the prices. I mean, they're not super expensive. You might want to check before you tuck in. And as I'm here during the festas, during the festivals in June, um I want to give you a warning on this one. It's sardine season, okay? And then And in general, I think there's a mistake that some tourists make when they order fish sometimes and it's here, especially sardines, and that is thinking that things will be filleted for you. Look, when you get robalo, dourada, seabream, sea bass when you're here is fantastic, but there's going to be bones. You will be deboning it yourself, okay? Also, with the sardinhas, you might have to take out the scales, you might have to take out some guts and stuff. So, just just don't make the mistake of thinking it's ready to go and just start eating. Hey guys, just know that you you you might have a little work to do when you're getting your fish here. I used to go to the market in Campo de Ourique and visit my friends up there and there's very few tourists that actually hang out at that market. But if you go around, there's all kinds of markets around town. It's not just that one and that's one thing I think a lot of bloggers kind of mess up is they make it seem like that's the end-all be-all of the markets of Lisbon. Well, yes, it's the biggest one really, but there's so many more out there to go explore. Now, tourists love coming to Lisbon because it has fantastic nightlife. There's all kinds of bars, restaurants you can go to. The thing is though, the mistake a lot of tourists make is thinking they can start partying early, and and that's the thing here in Lisbon, the locals don't go out to eat and drink until later, okay? So, they're going out to eat, they're going out to eat at 9:00, 10:00 at night, and then they're going out to Bairro Alto to go out and drink at like 11:00, 12:00, 1:00 at night, okay? Well, yeah, cuz the locals aren't going out till much later. Now, another mistake I see tourists make when they come here to Lisbon is they don't learn the song, the most important song in Lisbon. Another mistake people have when they come to Lisbon is they don't just head out to the park, head out to one of the little cafes that are in the park, the little kiosks that are there, grabbing a beer, having a snack, and just taking things in. They'll bring up robalo do dado sardinhas. They'll talk about the real local food. You want to have that cuz I've seen the TikTokers, they're like they seem to think that all they think people eat are pastel de nata. Well, again, remember you go to pastel, you want pastel de Belém. Wine sellers do some tours some tastings it's really fun then on our last day we took a day trip to the Duro Valley the birthplace of port wine for me this was a highlight of the whole trip it's such a Scenic part of the country absolutely breathtaking we found this great small group tour and our guide was actually a Somalia he brought us to two wineries that were totally different from each other in the best way oh oh it's really [Music] cool we went on a boat ride along the Duro River and and then checked out some awesome viewpoints of the Valley's terraced Vineyards and gorgeous Landscapes honestly this is a must do if you come to Porto non-negotiable I would even say next time I come back to Portugal though I'm definitely saving at least two or three nights for the Duro Valley it was [Music] beautiful we booked this trip on get your guide so I will leave a link in the description below or you can also scan the QR code in Porto we stayed at an apartment hotel called Victoria's Terrace Apartments it had really good reviews and I know why now it's in the perfect location right on the edge of the Oldtown area so perfectly walkable to everywhere but nice and quiet at night and the view from our balcony was actually better than one of the Hidden Gem viewpoints that was just down the street from us so that was pretty cool and the couple that runs this place are so kind and friendly they give us all kinds of recommend for places nearby like we went to a photo show that was 2 minutes away and it was an unforgettable [Music] evening there's this awesome little Hidden Gem Terrace bar called rooftop Flores go there for Sunset bevies and there's also great restaurants within 5 or 10 minutes I really liked C Tina 32 oh and I cannot forget franchina this is a Porto specific food it's just something you have to try it's bread and two or three kinds of meat and there's cheese and an egg on top and a tomato beer sauce we found awesome ones at this restaurant that I cannot pronounce but their whole menu looked great as well overall I think 2 and 1/2 days in Porto was enough I would absolutely stay 3 or 4 days if I had more time but I think you do need at least one and a half to two full days to really experience the city plus a full day dedicated to the duroro valley on day five instead of taking the train straight to Lisbon we actually hired a driver and then that way we were able to turn the transfer day into a full sightseeing day and this was honestly one of the best decisions of our trip so I'll leave the link to vur where we found our guide and driver he picked us up from our hotel in Porto at 9:00 a.m. and then we arrived in Lisbon by 6:00 p.m. where we made three stops along the way first we stopped in AO which is known as the Venice of Portugal because of its canals and colorful boats it's a pretty little town as well with lots of beautiful buildings in art Nuvo style it's also known for a traditional Suite called ooes not sure how to pronounce it but something we learned was you always have to try the local pastry when you're in Portugal our next stop was nazer a coastal town famous for its massive waves that attract Surfers from all over the world usually from November to February is where you can see the highest waves on Earth sometimes reaching as high as 30 m while we weren't visiting in big wave season the viewpoints overlooking the ocean and the beach are absolutely breathtaking and definitely worth the stop the village itself is also quite picturesque and the pink marble cathedral was definitely one of the highlights finally we stopped in obidos a small medieval Town enclosed by Castle stone walls that you can actually walk on top of I loved this little town so much it's cobblestone streets flower lined alleys historic charm little shops it was just so adorable and it's also known for its Cherry lure Ginger which is served in little chocolate cups and there's little ginger stands every 20 or 30 feet they're only about one or one and a half years per shot so I definitely recommend trying out a few I would have loved to spend at least half a day here in obidos next time maybe even stay in the castle which is now actually a fourstar hotel so if you only have time for two stops I would probably skip AO and if I only had time to visit one place I would definitely choose obidos we then arrived in Lisbon where we checked into our hotel and stayed for the next 4 nights we stayed at this apart hotel called Ando living once again I booked it because of the amazing reviews and they were totally right this hotel was in the perfect location in the baija neighborhood so we were within walking distance or easy public transport to all the main attractions in the old neighborhoods it was also nice and quiet at night it was so clean new and modern reasonably priced and the little welcome package of wine and goodies was a lovely touch and literally around the corner was the Green Street area which is three blocks of little authentic bars and restant resturants that open up in the evening it's kind of similar to Pink street but the better more authentic Hidden Gem among locals so then we spent the next two full days exploring Lisbon on our first day we took a halfday tuck tuck tour this was a fantastic way to get introduced to the city our guide took us through Lisbon's famous neighborhoods Alama bisha bario elto and chiato Lisbon is also filled with viewpoints or Mirad duros and our guide took us to some of the best ones for the best views of the city he also took great photos of us which turned out to be some of my favorites from the whole trip we finished our tutic tour in bam home to the BM Tower built to defend Lisbon against possible attacks and invasions and it's one of the most unique monuments in Lisbon and there's nothing quite like it in other European cities as well there's the gonos monastery which is a gorgeous Portuguese Gothic building that was built in the 17th century this is also home to the birthplace of the famous pases de balm the original recipe of pastel denata everyone said these were the best of the best the Portuguese custard Tarts and yes I can confirm they are in A League of Their Own we booked this trip through get your guide so I'll leave the link below to the one that we booked or you can scan the QR code I also just want to say that none of this trip was sponsored we paid for everything ourselves from the hotels to the guides to the drivers to the restaurants everything Lisbon is a great City to just wander get lost take in the sights snap some fun photos and stop for food and bees along the way and that's exactly what we did we really liked the carel rooftop bar for Sunset as well and if you like espresso martinis you have to visit cassa do pastel deata leor for a special lisban Twist on this yummy cocktail [Music] we also took in another foto show the old neighborhoods here in Lisbon is where this originated foto is a traditional style of Portuguese music characterized by mournful tunes and lyrics often expressing a sense of longing Nostalgia or sadness I would say catching a photo show is a must do we chose to do dinner and the photo show at Bohemia LX but you can also find smaller venues and stop in and have maybe a glass of wine while you listen to the Beautiful music [Music] all right now here are a few things that I thought were a little overrated in lisbin first was the timeout Market to me this was just a food court but perhaps if you're in town for only a few hours maybe from a cruise and you want to try a bunch of different Portuguese Foods this would probably be a good stop I also wasn't crazy about pink Street maybe I'm too old it was loud busy and full of influencers and waiting in line for the Santa useda lift it was really cool to look at it's very interesting architecture for sure but you can actually find stairs to go up for free or you can also get a great view of it from the carel rooftop I probably could have used an extra day in Lisbon but because we saw Porto which I kind of preferred the more laid-back vibe there 2 days was enough for me in Lisbon I will say Lisbon was very busy when we were there in mid October so I can't even imagine how busy it gets in the summer months our last and Lisbon was dedicated to cintra a fairy tale Town nestled in the Hills about 45 minutes from the city this is one of the most popular day trips from Lisbon and a place that you absolutely have to see with your own eyes there are several stunning palaces to visit here and you won't have time to see them all in one day so you will have to choose we visited two of the most famous palaces starting with the colorful Penna Palace which looks like something straight out of a story book it's got colorful Towers an Eclectic [Music] architecture we then toured the intricate Kint de regula a romantic estate with beautiful gardens underground tunnels and the famous initiation well we booked a private tour and this was absolutely worth every penny because cintra is huge there is so much more than just a few palaces to visit our guide not only brought us everywhere but came with us and explained all the history and all the stories behind everything we were looking at I am so glad we hired a guide this whole area is breathtaking but also quite overwhelming especially with the crowds there were so many people without a guide that were kind of just aimlessly wandering around and a lot of them were constantly trying to listen into what our guide was sharing with us so I'll leave a link to the guide that we booked if you booked with him let him know Al Smalls sent you and if he's not available I also left links for a couple other highly rated guides and tours for our final leg of the trip we took a 45-minute flight from Lisbon to Pharaoh a city in the algar we got ourselves a rental car and then drove to caruo our home base for the last four nights we chose caruo because it's centrally located which made it really easy to explore the other towns beaches and hiking trails along the algar coast the town itself is super Charming with its whitewashed buildings and a beautiful Coastline we stayed at a six suet Guest House Bed and Breakfast called Kint do ores when I tell you it's one of the best accommodations I have ever stayed in I am not exaggerating it's run by the sweetest family who are so kind and full of great recommendations for what to do in the area each morning guests are treated to a massive breakfast spread and any type of coffee that you want there's a large heated pool with all kinds of room for sunbathing plenty of common areas to hang out and relax in and all the rooms have private patios with kitchens I will 100% be booking a vacation just to stay here again we only had four nights and I wish we had 10 more one thing we quickly realized after getting to the Algarve was that four nights was nowhere near enough but here were the highlights for us in that time one of our favorite spots was algar SEO this is a spot with Incredible rock formations and natural Gros all along the coast you can take some amazing photos and even Explore all the little hidden caves it's only 5 minutes from the hotel as well we also hiked 8 km of the seven hanging valleys Trail which offers some of the best coastal views in all of Portugal the trail runs along the cliffs above the ocean passing by Scenic viewpoints hidden Coes and secluded beaches this is a must do and was a highlight of the whole trip for me another highlight was kaying to the benel C caves we were able to get so much closer than the bigger boat tours we took a guided tour and we saw so many other caves while we kayaked along the coastline highly recommend doing this but it's definitely a bit of a workout so be prepared for that we spent some time visiting some of the most famous and beautiful beaches and postcard views near Lagos in albuera each beach in The Algarve has its own Vibe and so exploring a few of them really adds to the experience [Music] there's also a lot of cute towns and Villages all over I really loved farago an adorably picturesque town with whitewashed houses built in the Hillside and flowers all through the streets also a great spot for souvenir shopping overall it was an amazing trip and I think 12 days was a good amount of time to cover a lot of ground without feeling too rushed especially because we made the most of our travel days I went on this trip with my mom and sister and we all took one carry-on bag and one personal item each and that was definitely the way to go these are the bags that we used here that fit on all the airlines that we flew I'll leave links to the bags but I also made a video talking about them more in depth and we also used sling bags I used the large Sling from Nomad Lane my sister used the Metro Sling from monos and my mom used her belt bag from Lululemon each of these were perfect size and really nice and secure so I'll leave links to the those as well I also put this itinerary up on my travel blog with a little more detail if you want to find out more and keep checking back because I'm going to be adding more posts about Portugal I'm also working on a Portugal pocket guide that will be full of all my tips and research and recommendations so I'll announce that here on my channel when it's ready I hope you found this video helpful let me know in the comments when your upcoming trip to Portugal is thank you so much for watching and I'll see you in the next one [Music]

videos.title: Top Things to Do in Lisbon, Portugal | Ultimate Travel Guide 2026
videos.description:
Welcome to the Ultimate Travel Guide 2026 to Lisbon, Portugal—your essential resource for discovering all the top things to do in this captivating capital city.
From iconic viewpoints and historic neighborhoods to vibrant culture, coastal beauty, and unforgettable cuisine, Lisbon offers endless experiences for every traveler.

In this Lisbon Travel Guide, we’ll take you through the absolute best things to do in and around the city: must-see attractions, hidden gems, and unforgettable spots that reveal the true spirit of Portugal’s capital.

You’ll also find practical tips to make your visit smooth and stress-free, including the smartest ways to buy tickets, how to navigate Lisbon’s hills and transport system, and insight into the surrounding region’s highlights.

Whether you’re planning your first visit or returning to dive deeper into Lisbon’s charm, this guide is designed to help you make the most of your adventure.
Let’s begin exploring the very best of Lisbon, Portugal!

▬▬▬▬▬▬ T I M E S T A M P S   ▬▬▬▬▬▬

videos.transcript: Hi, I am Martijn and welcome to Portugal. This is Lisbon. The Portuguese capital. In this travel guide you see the best things to do and see. From a beautiful monastery and castle... ...to ancient neighborhoods full with history and music... ...the best viewpoints and nearby beaches... ...to everything in between. Lisbon International Airport is located 7 kilometers outside the city center. And to travel to the city, you can use a taxi. The journey takes about 15 minutes and cost between €15 and €20. There's the metro red line to the downtown area for only €2. And of course, the aerobus, which stops at major landmarks and hotels. Let's kick off our Lisbon journey with a breathtaking monument from Portugal's golden age, the Jerónimos Monastery. Built in 1501, this UNESCO World Heritage site was funded by the profits of the spice trade and discoveries of explorers like Vasco da Gama. The monks of the order of St. Jerome once prayed here for the safety of the sailors embarking on journeys to unknown worlds. The building is a masterpiece of Manueline architecture, a uniquely Portuguese style that blends Gothic, Moorish, and maritime motifs. Inside lies the tomb of Vasco da Gama, the man who linked Europe to India by sea forever changing global trade. Close to the monastery, standing guard over the Tagus River is the Belém Tower. Built between 1515 and 1521, this was the first and last site sailors would see before long trips across the Atlantic. Over the centuries, it served also as a prison and a customs house. And keep in mind that the building is being restored right now and will be reopened in 2026. And until then, you see this. Just a few steps along the waterfront, you will find a striking monument to the discoveries, shaped like the front of a ship. And this monument from the 20th century honors Portugal's great navigators. They are all carved in stone as leading the nation forward. It's not just a monument, but a reminder of how Portugal's tiny fleets reshape the world. And you can also stand on top for a beautiful view. The beating heart of old Lisbon is Alfama, a labyrinth of history. Alfama is a survivor. It's one of the few neighborhoods that withstood a devastating earthquake of 1755. Its streets are a mosaic of ceramic tiles, laundry lines, and small bars where Fado singers put their soul into haunting melodies. Roman walls, Moorish influences, and medieval alleys blend seamlessly. Lisbon is a city of seven hills with countless viewpoints and one of them is Santa Luzia. Also located in Alfama district with beautiful views from the rooftops all the way to the Tagus River. Perched above Alfama stands the São Jorge Castle, a crown jewel of the city. This fortress has watched over Lisbon for more than 2,000 years. From Roman settlers to Moorish rulers and Portuguese kings. Explore its towers and ramparts where peacocks roam freely and imagine the sieges and battles fought here. The castle's vantage point offers one of Lisbon's most breathtaking panoramas. And always book your tickets in advance to skip the line. When you walk down from the castle, you will see the Lisbon Cathedral, also known as the Sé. Built in 1147, right after the Christian reconquest of Lisbon, this Romanesque masterpiece feels like a fortress of faith. Step inside to uncover treasures dating back to Roman times. Get ready to experience one of Europe's biggest squares surrounded by a vibrant neighborhood full of life and charm. One of Lisbon's most classic experiences is a ride on Tram 28. These bright yellow trams have been rattling through Lisbon's hills for over a century. The route is like a moving postcard passing through beautiful neighborhoods. And yes, it's a ride full of charm, but also a lot of people with long waiting times. So, my advice is to take line 12 less busy and almost the same experience. You already know that the big earthquake in 1755 destroyed a big part of the city. also this area Baixa. And it was rebuilt as one of Europe's first grid-planned neighborhoods. Wide boulevards, ornate balconies and the famous black and white wave pattern pavements define its charm. it's also a popular shopping area with a lot of restaurants. They can be pretty touristy so if you don't want to sit with the crowds just head a few streets further where it's much calmer. This square Rossio has witnessed royal celebrations, bull fights and political revolutions. Today, it's a lively hub lined with cafes and restaurants. Nearby, the Santa Justa Lift offers a vertical shortcut to the city’s best rooftop views. Designed in 1902 by a student of Gustav Eiffel. Once powered by steam, it now runs on electricity, but it still retains its 19th century charm. In just a moment, we'll dive into stunning tiles and a market that's a feast for the senses. At the edge of the river, Lisbon opens up in one of the most impressive squares in the world, Praça do Comércio. This square was once the seat of royal power until the 1755 earthquake destroyed the palace that stood here. Rebuilt in neocclassical style, and the yellow arcaded buildings wrap around the square like royal arms. In the center stands a statue of King Jose the first on horseback. He was the Portuguese king between 1750 and 1777. And from here you can easily walk to the waterfront. No trip to Lisbon is complete without discovering the beautiful Portuguese tiles in a National Tile Museum. Housed in a former convent, it tells the story of azulejos (the ceramic tiles). From their Moorish origins to modern-day designs. Inside you can also visit this beautiful church. It's a masterpiece a massive 23 m long panorama of the city before the earthquake of 1755. A unique place to eat or drink something is the Timeout Market located in a 19th century Mercado da Ribeira. Since 2014, it has brought together the city's best chefs and traditional dishes under one roof. You'll discover a lineup of around 36 to 40 restaurants and kiosks, including creations by renowned chefs. The atmosphere is lively, especially in the afternoons and evenings when locals gather for drinks and shared plates. For a taste of modern Lisbon, you have to visit Parque das Nações, the futuristic part of the city. Built for Expo '98. It's a riverside promenade lined with modern sculptures, cable cars and world-class Lisbon Oceanarium. This is one of Europe's largest indoor aquariums. Its central tank holds over five million liters of seawater. The area is a favorite spot for families, cyclists, and anyone who loves a mix of art and nature by the water. In moments, you will discover an architectural icon and a vibrant nightlife neighborhood. Stretching along the Tagus River, the 25th of April Bridge is one of the most iconic landmarks in Lisbon. Opened in 1966 and its red color and design can remind you of the Golden Gate Bridge in San Francisco. And that's not weird. It was built by the same American company. And it connects Lisbon to another place, Almada on the South Bank. On the Almada site, you will find the impressive Cristo Rei statue inspired by Rio de Janeiro's Christ the Redeemer. Standing 110 meters above the river and the monument was completed in 1959. From its viewing platform, you can enjoy panoramic views of the bridge, the city skyline, and the sparkling river below. My advice is to visit this place in the morning with the sun in your back to capture the best pictures of yourself and the city. On the edge of one of Lisbon's steep hills, the bohemian heart of the city is Bairro Alto. And this area dates back to the 16th century and is a mixture of old world world charm and modern energy. A neighborhood where narrow cobbled streets, colorful tiled buildings, and beautiful balconies set the scene. By day, it feels like a quiet, charming village, perfect for exploring quirky shops... ...cafes, and small art galleries. But when the sun sets, the neighborhood transforms into one of Lisbon's liveliest nightlife districts. With a maze of bars, restaurants, and clubs. Over here, you will also find a great viewpoint, São Pedro de Alcântara. It's time to visit not only a museum, but also an architectural statement. The Museum of Art, Architecture, and Technology. Exhibitions here blur the line between art and technology, exploring themes like climate change and digital culture. The mission is to host contemporary exhibitions and stimulating programs. Offering space for national and international artists, architects, and thinkers. The museum consists of several buildings that you can visit with the same ticket. The rooftop is a favorite sunset spot with the 25th of April bridge stretching across the horizon. Lisbon is not only known for the beautiful architecture and history, but also for the stunning coastline. While the city itself has no beaches, the surrounding areas offer plenty of them. About 20 km away lies Praia de Carcavelos. It's famous for its wide sandy shore, popular surf spots, and lively atmosphere. Making it a favorite destination for both locals and tourists. For creativity and cool vibes, make your way to the LX Factory. Once an abandoned industrial site, this area is now busting with street art, bars, concept shops, and some of Lisbons trendiest restaurants. The highlights, a beautiful bookstore inside an old printing press where books rise like towers under iron beams. If you're looking for a peaceful escape from the city boss, then head to park at Eduardo the 7th. This is the city's largest central park named after the king of England who visited Portugal in 1903. And this green space stretches over 25 hectares. Offering wide lawns, perfectly trimmed hedges, and sweeping views all the way to the Tagus River. Hidden in the heart of the park is a botanical garden. This is a beautiful oasis filled with tropical and subtropical plants. It feels like stepping into a secret jungle in the middle of the city. What is the best time for a visit? I think spring, March till June or autumn, September and October. Perfect weather conditions, less crowded and also a nice period to explore the hills on foot. And also the summer peak July and August. It's hot and crowded. Please give a thumbs up. Thank you so much. When you like this Lisbon travel guide. And if you don't want to miss new episodes, just subscribe on my channel. It's totally free and it enriches your view on the whole world.
